sentences.text: I was in [?
Auschwitz. ?] I mean, things didn't go well.
And already, starting in '42, where-- you know, after end of '42.
But we heard from the guards, they'd talk and they think the war is going to be over soon and things like that.
And--
And-- and from the outside.
I mean, the underground got all the information from the outside as well.
And so the people in leading positions or in permanent positions in the camp knew what was going on, because they had connections with the outside.
From them.
That's right.
Yeah, well, they-- they had contact with civil workers, and they are in contact, some of them, with the-- et cetera.
From them, yes.
Yes.
The only trouble is, as things got worse for the German, they-- they-- they accelerated their killings.
How would I know that?
We knew that transports were coming in, one after another, more than anybody else from Hungaria, for instance.
And the chimneys were burning like hell.
We could-- we could smell the-- the sweet flesh of the burning chimney all over Auschwitz and Birkenau.
Oh, yes.
And we could see the chimneys on the-- on the-- on the sky, we could see the fire going up on the chimneys.
We knew when the chimneys are really firing and when there are just one or two, little bit, you know?
So they were all firing at that particular time.
So we knew that the transport's coming in.
We also knew from the transports who come in, the Hungarian transports, into the camp.
So younger ones.
And we knew how many arrived.
And where are the others?
That was '43, '44. '44. '44 was the biggest killing going on.
Because that was already-- you see, in '44, as I tell you now, was already the final solution in [INAUDIBLE] Berlin, decided by Eichmann.
Yeah, but after that, they start really firing.
Yeah.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] To see the ash of the chimneys are very fine.
Little black ash, and it comes on your clothing.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Like a dust from a volcano.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And you could smell the sweetness in the air, of burnt flesh.
Very [INAUDIBLE].
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We knew.
At that stage, my mind was sane, my job was good, my health was good.
So somehow along the line, I hoped it.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We also had a brothel in-- in Auschwitz, I don't know if you knew about that.
A brothel.
Jewish women.
Kapos.
Non-Jews.
Germans.
[SPEAKING GERMAN] Not the-- not the high officers.
They had their own somewhere else.
Guards.
They got coupons.
That means if a kapo does well, that gets a coupon.
He gets points, bonuses, so he could go to the brothel.
Those women were exchanged every six months.
Up the chimney.
We know.
Well, we knew that the Russian forces were going on Raschau.
Because we had prisoners coming in full uniform from Raschau, in Polish uniforms, into Auschwitz.
They were all shot.
Generals and all sorts of things.
Because the Poles hoped that the Russians will liberate them, and they started.
And as they started, they were let down by the Russians purposely.
So they came in into the camp.
Army, army personnel.
Polish--
Yeah.
And they were going straight to block 11, from block 11 were trucks out, finished.
In uniforms.
In Polish uniforms.
So we knew that the-- the Russians were not far.
And we knew that the Germans were on the run already then.
Not until the last minutes.
In January, we saw the red light on-- on the-- on the river who went to through Katowice, near Krakau, on the other side.
I forgot the name of the river.
We saw the artillery fire, red.
And that night, that night, we all got out.
They told us all to get out of the barracks, and the rumors were that the whole camp is dynamited.
They wanted to blow us up before they leave, but they didn't have the time.
So they took us with them as hostages.
So everybody, disregarding who it was, had to line up.
Whoever could walk-- whoever couldn't walk stayed in the Krankenbau, that is the sickness hospital, yeah.
And the old-- and, you know, not the old.
There was no old there there.
The ones who couldn't walk and things like that, who were beyond, they just left them there.
And they were mining the whole camp, you know, [BACKROUND NOISE] that the camp was mined.
And they lined us all up, got us out of the barracks.
It was snowing that day, full with snow.
And it was the 17th of January, 1945.
And we were given one bread under the arm-- a full bread, not a quarter.
One bread.
A half a-- half a loaf of margarine, and a little jar of-- of-- of jam.
And a piece of sausage, about a quarter of a sausage.
We filled up our pockets.
We were given one blanket and off we went.
Out of the camp.
No music this time.
And we went out of the camp in columns, in sections.
A few thousand at the time.
And all the SS guards were there, with their dogs, with their things, and we left the camp.
One after the other, [? were to ?] hundreds of thousands of people in the camp.
But we all got evacuate.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And we were going to the ramp, to the train.
And we were-- they pushed us in the wagons.
And the train was going, and some of the wagons were open wagons.
There was no roof on it.
You know, where they put coal and wood in it, that type of wagons?
And then we were rolling for about a half a night or night.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And then we were stopping.
And they screamed, everybody out.
Nobody was moving.
Half of them were frozen to death.
And we went through bodies.
I went out through bodies up on top.
The other ones were frozen, dead.
And whoever alive-- was alive, we went there, and then we had to run on the spot to warm ourselves up.
We were lining up back to back to rub our lungs to get the blood going, rubbing like that.
And then we were start walking.
And they marched us in the nighttime, only night, with-- with guards on the right and guards on the left.
No.
No.
And we could hear the shooting, the artillery, still.
So we couldn't have been too far.
And sometimes we had to stop for hours to let the German tanks go through.
They went through the front, German tank columns.
And on the death of our march column was an execution squad.
There were about 20 SS who shot people all the time.
Whoever couldn't walk, stayed behind, too weak, they gave them a shot, put them on the truck.
Yeah.
When the truck was full, the truck was going in the forest, they put petrol over and burned them.
No.
I wasn't in the forest.
We knew it.
We could smell it.
And we knew that the truck with the SS where the petrol was going in the forest.
So we knew.
See, when you are in a camp, you develop a instinct.
You know, you know things.
You know what they're doing, you know how they're acting, you know what's happening.
And people had diarrhea.
And more-- it-- it went right through.
What you call cholera.
We start to develop cholera.
And cholera is you-- you-- you've got diarrhea to death.
And you have to-- all the time you're running empty.
And you have to put your trousers down and do it and do it.
And you stay behind the column.
And when you come on the end to do it, you get the shot.
Because the column is moving.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That's it.
And that's how we got smaller and smaller and smaller.
And that's how people fell down.
They couldn't walk anymore.
And their shoes, their shoes they couldn't use anymore.
And we were looking of people who were falling down to get their shoes.
Because he-- to him, it's no good anymore.
To me it is.
So a guy who got good shoes on who could hardly walk anymore, I watched him.
I watched him that long that he fell down, then I took his shoes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's a survival of the fittest.
Just simple as that.
Nights.
In daytime, they locked us in barns, at the farmer.
There were just came to a farm, they told the farmer to go away, just-- they-- they put-- they put SS around the barn, and we went and slept in the barn in the stall.
Nighttime, they got us out of the barn, we marched again.
And the kapos were marching with us next to it, and-- and the-- and the kitchen, a field kitchen, I was walking next to the field kitchen.
And the field kitchen was pushed by prisoners, physically.
We had to cook, they had to keep us alive.
Oh yeah.
Oh, yeah, yeah.
You see, we were-- we-- we were the hostages.
They-- you see-- well, they want to make deals later on with us, with the-- with the-- with Allies.
I know the route we took.
We took-- we went from Auschwitz into Czechoslovakia, from Poland into Czechoslovakia.
From Czechoslovakia, we went to Austria.
Walked.
Walked.
I ended up first in Gross Rosen and then in Flossenburg.
In Gross Rosen, we were-- we came on [? weak ?] There was no more position.
It doesn't matter if you're in a fire brigade, it doesn't matter.
We-- we were all treated like-- from the other prisoners, like intruding.
Intruders.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So a lot of people were eliminated.
By the-- by the local kapos, which were non-- not all that-- in Gross Rosen, there was no Jews.
No.
No Jewish prisoners.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] All the prisoners were Ukrainians, Poles, and things like that.
Except when they evacuated Auschwitz, then they came in.
A lot of people got killed.
Beaten to death on the showers.
Water hoses in their mouth, and hold them till they blow up.
A lot of things.
Anyhow, I-- I spoke Russian.
So I spoke to those Ukrainians.
I pretended to be one of them.
Beg your pardon?
In Gross Rosen, about a week.
Not quite a week.
A week, maybe.
And then from there to Flossenburg, on top of a mountain.
Flossenburg is right on top-- I've got it here on a map [INAUDIBLE].
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's on top of the mountain.
It's in Bavaria, near Nuremberg, not far from Nuremberg.
By foot.
When you go up the mountain, you don't see anything.
All you see is clouds around you.
Probably another week.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And a lot of people died on the way.
And as I mentioned to you in a previous, I marched with a fellow called Jacob who was a hangman in block-- block 10.
Block 11, sorry.
And he was pretty bad off.
He was a very heavy man and he had-- and he couldn't walk anymore.
He was-- we had to carry him.
We carried him, he was good.
He was a hangman in Auschwitz, but he was a good man.
He saved a lot of people.
And whatever didn't, you know, he passed information.
Risking his life, too.
And in Flossenburg, that was even worse than in Gross Rosen.
That was a hell.
Anyway, we got out of there, luckily.
Hmm?
Same thing happened.
They killed people.
They killed people who survived the march.
The inmates.
Croatia, Ukraine.
They killed a lot of Russians, too.
Non-Jews.
They were with us, too.
Some of them were.
They took Russian POWs and put them in the camps.
A POW [INAUDIBLE] because, you see-- well, it doesn't concern me.
But all the orders was in-- all except British and American POWs came under the SS, under Himmler.
That's why they came into our camp.
And--
Yeah.
Of the-- of the atrocities.
They were outside the camp.
They were not in the camp.
In the camp was hardly any Germans.
Ukrainians, Croatians, Polish.
Latvians.
No, the prisoners.
They were laughing.
They were stirring up the prisoners to do the-- the kapos and things like that.
All were kapos, and kapos, they were doing the dirty work.
The Germans were outside the camp.
They were like untouchables.
They're giving the commands but, you know.
In Flossenburg, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I remember one German, Obersturmfuhrer, gave us a speech on our arrival.
He said, the only way out of here-- the only way out of here, he said, is arbeit macht Frei.
Work makes free.
But you are bastards.
You don't want to work, so you'll never be free.
And then he says, [SPEAKING GERMAN] love to the fatherland.
Well, you bastards, you have no love to the fatherland, so you have got no chance.
He said whoever is here in honesty, he said whoever is here honest only lives four weeks, maximum.
And if you live longer here in this camp than four weeks, you are not honest.
You steal bread.
So you have to be exterminated.
That was his speech.
He said, in here, first comes the German, he said.
The German prisoners comes first, he said.
And then comes nothing, he said.
And then come the Poles and the Ukrainians and all that.
And then it comes a big sea in the Sahara, he said, and then come the Jews.
That was his speech of [INAUDIBLE].
I remember that.
And--
About 10 days.
Then they started to march us again.
And I could feel I couldn't take it anymore.
I could feel I'm coming to the end of my strength.
And I thought-- I-- I could feel that I finish up on that truck in the back.
The last truck.
So in Bavaria, in the hills, we went around the hill.
Our column, that's all I know.
Our column.
It was raining, raining cats and dogs.
We were in the forest, left and right.
On the left to me there were guards.
They had their rain caps over their head and they had the rifle underneath, sticking out.
And it was raining that night, and I knew that's the night.
We were marching in columns of six.
When I moved out, they moved in, the others.
And I saw that guard on the left going to take a cigarette and asked the other guard for a light-- for a light.
I took my blanket over my head and I put my stick, I had a walking stick, like a-- and I put that up in the back as a rifle.
And I made four steps to the left in the same line than the guards.
And in the silhouette, I looked no different than the guards.
I had my old-- my wet blanket over my head, I had the stick in my back, and I made three steps on the side.
And all of a sudden, I saw the [? real ?] guard coming up towards me.
And in half broken German I said, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
In German, go over there, he's got a lighter.
And he went away from me, and I rolled down the hill on the left.
I rolled and I rolled and I rolled in the snow.
And then [? kept-- ?] and that was a steep hill.
And I knew that that night they couldn't get the dogs on me, because dogs can't smell in the wet weather.
They lose their smell in the rain.
In Bavaria, it was snowing.
February, March.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I rolled down the hill, and then all of a sudden, I looked up and hell broke loose.
They were counting.
See, before they go everywhere, they always keep on counting.
And when they finish counting, you can march.
So they counted and counted again, there was one missing.
And hell broke loose.
And spotlights, dogs screaming, dogs running everywhere.
Not down.
They didn't go far, it was too steep for them.
You see, I was trying to push myself up because I got hold up by plants.
So I pushed myself away going to roll further.
I was heading-- hiding under the leaves and lying there.
And then finally I saw the column marching, away.
And then I started to get up, started to [? call, ?] started to walk as I could, stagger.
And then I went across.
And on a-- on-- on a hill, on a plateau, I came on the other side.
I saw them again.
See what I did is they walked around, and I walked across.
And I saw the whole column.
I ran quick back again.
They disappeared.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then I was in a forest.
The next day, I was starving.
I was trying to get a rabbit.
Anyhow, I went up a tree.
Then I went up the tree, and I slept there.
In a tree, I tied myself up.
Oh, in the meantime, I found a dead Russian.
A dead Russian prisoner with-- with-- with a paint in the back, with a head in the river.
I undressed him, took it off, I took my uniform off, and I put his uniform on.
And mine I threw away.
So I had a Russian POW uniform instead of his-- instead of mine.
And then I sat in the trees and all of a sudden, I woke up.
There were Hungarian SS on bicycles cooking goulash.
I nearly fell in the pot, I gave myself up.
[LAUGHS] Nearly, not quite.
Anyhow, they left after about 15, 20 minutes, and I licked out the pots.
And then I saw a farmhouse.
In the meantime, I acquired a knife, by the way.
From the Russian.
[INAUDIBLE].
And I saw a woman coming from a chicken farm with eggs.
And I hide behind a tree, and I put the knife on her here.
I took the basket of [? trees ?] away and a chicken.
A German woman.
And I don't know what I did, something I did.
I think I hit her on the head or something.
She fell, she fainted.
I took the basket with the eggs, and I ran in the forest.
I ate eggs and I had terrible diarrhea.
And the chicken I cooked, but not in a fire.
I cooked it in-- in-- in a-- in a solution, in-- in-- in-- like a-- like a lime.
In lime.
Gets hot after a while.
And I ate it, half cooked.
And that was it.
And I kept on running in the forest.
[SIGHS] I walked and I walked and I walked.
And I stole.
Nighttime, the-- alarmed the police, you know.
In the nighttime I pinched the farmhouses.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then one day, I was sitting on the trees.
Always sitting on trees.
Oh, by the way, I heard that the column was-- they had finished up the column from others who had escaped.
They put a truck-- they put the canvas out on the truck and put the machine gun on it.
The last two days, or three days of the column, that same column.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And all of a sudden, the trees were going down where I was.
I hear the cracking of the trees.
I couldn't believe it.
Big, big cones of trees were go-- and I saw tanks.
Big, huge tanks.
I'd never seen an American tank in my life.
I thought they were Russian tanks because I saw the star.
But it was an American star, not a Russian star.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I came like that towards them.
And I start speaking Russian to them.
And they stopped, nobody came out.
They put the guns at me.
And I start to scream and show them the sign, Auschwitz.
Jew.
Juden.
[NON-ENGLISH] Nothing happens.
Now, all of a sudden, the door opened up and three guys came out, two-- two black fellows and one-- one officer in the jeep, and came towards me and put me the gun in the back.
And I tried-- tried to speak in very broken English.
I want to talk to officer.
I want to talk to officer.
I'm prisoner, prisoner from German.
No, they-- they-- they didn't say a word.
They put me in a jeep, gave me a chewing gum to shut up, and drove me back with the jeep.
And I came to headquarters, and I screamed, I want to see your officer.
Then I realized they were Americans, when I heard them talk English.
There was a guy standing there like that.
It was pouring rain, mind you, and, you know, I didn't know who he was.
I didn't realize he was a colonel.
I mean, you know.
With me, colonels have got fancy uniforms and, you know, big hats and plenty of silver and gold.
He was a colonel, American colonel.
And he looked at me, and he said, clean him up.
They cleaned me up.
They gave me khaki shirt, a khaki pants, and boots.
And they still-- nobody talks to me.
And I was a prisoner.
They gave me soup to eat.
I showed him my sign, they tell me to shut up.
Said ?] nice people, not any better.
So finally, they brought me in a room.
They were sitting and their jacket with a-- with a thing on it.
Two-- two stars.
I don't know what he was.
Three stars.
He said, what's your name?
And I said, George Ginsburg.
In German, they said, [SPEAKING GERMAN]?
I said, George Ginsberg.
He said, where are you from?
Belgium.
They never said a word.
And one fellow comes in, he says his name, Colonel Friedman, Joseph Friedman.
Colonel Joseph Friedman.
I said, ah, you-- shut up, sit down.
I couldn't understand anything.
And that was the start of the interview of the whole thing.
And I said, I'm a Jew.
They didn't believe me.
They didn't believe me, how come I-- I'm alive?
They have seen bodies of prisoners lying on the route as they came.
So how come that I'm alive, and I'm in a Russian uniform, and I speak, fluently, German?
And I speak Russian, and so on.
The story is this.
Now, I know the story, but I come to it in a minute.
They asked me if I can say Shema Yisrael.
I said yes.
I said it.
And they said, call Captain Klein.
Captain Klein came in.
The moment I said it, [SOBBING] the colonel was jumping up and kissing me.
Just-- I have never had such emotion a moment in my life.
What happens, now I know.
Is that finished?
